title: Chapter Twenty-One: Aftermath
content: The morning after the livestream, Bly Manor becomes a crime scene.

Not literally. No police came. Henry's lawyers saw to that — I imagine them working through the night, silk-tongued and pitiless, turning subpoenas into suggestions and warrants into polite requests. By dawn, the whole thing had been reclassified. Not an emergency. A dispute. A family matter. Nothing to see.
But atmospherically? The house knows what it is.

The silence is different. Thicker. It doesn't settle; it presses. I lie in bed and listen to it — the absence of Mrs. Grose's footsteps in the kitchen, the missing clatter of the breakfast routine, the way the pipes don't groan because nobody's running water. Everyone is in their rooms. Nobody comes down. The manor holds us all in separate compartments like a specimen drawer, and for the first time since I arrived, no one is performing.
I reach for my phone.
Reddit is a war zone.

I scroll through the threads with my thumb trembling, and the response splits three ways before my eyes. The Believers are archived and multiplying — they've screenshot everything, clipped the footage, circled Miles's mouth forming the word *look* in the moment before Flora turned to camera. The Skeptics are louder, demanding metadata, chain of custody, forensic audio analysis of background hum. They want proof the footage wasn't staged, and they want it in formats I can't provide.
But it's the third faction that stops me cold.
They call themselves the Flora Defenders.

*Leave that child alone.* The top comment has twelve thousand upvotes. *She's FOURTEEN. She didn't ask to be on camera. Whoever streamed this exploited a minor for internet points and should be investigated.*
I read it twice. Three times. The words don't change.
Maeve used a fourteen-year-old as a prop in her war, and the internet has noticed.
I didn't mean to.
But I did.

I put Flora on camera without her consent. She was performing — I can see that now, watching the clip someone's already enhanced and re-uploaded. The hitch in her voice. The way her eyes tracked to where Miles stood off-screen, checking. She was doing what she'd been trained to do, and I captured it, and I broadcast it, and now fourteen thousand strangers are dissecting the compliance of a child they'll never have to look in the face.

The compliance I've been performing — docile cousin, cooperative student, good sport about the cold and the locked doors and the games — was itself a form of exploitation. I made Flora a mirror for my own performance, and I didn't see it because I was too busy seeing through her.
She was the glass. I used her as a window.
I am becoming what I hate.

I set the phone face-down on the nightstand. The screen flashes once — a notification I don't check — and then the room is just the room again. Gray light. Old plaster. The faint mineral smell of the radiator, which no one has turned on.
I don't cry. I don't deserve to.
The note comes at ten.
I hear nothing. No footsteps, no knock. Just the soft friction of paper sliding across hardwood, and then silence resealing itself like water over a stone.
I wait a full minute before I get up.
The note is handwritten — blue ink, careful cursive, the letters slightly too even. Practiced. Like penmanship drills from another century.
*Come to the library at noon. We need to discuss terms.*
No signature. None needed.

I hold the paper between my fingers and feel its weight, which is nothing, which is everything. The cheap stock. The slight roughness where the nib caught on the fiber. The crease where he folded it in half and then thought better and pressed it flat again — I can see the ghost of the first fold, a centimeter off from the second.
He planned this. He's already planning.
The Game hasn't ended.

I sit on the edge of my bed and understand, with the clarity of a bone setting, that the livestream was Round 7's climax. I won the round. I went public. I forced the board into the open and made the players visible. But the Game resets between rounds. The board clears. The pieces return to start. And Miles — Miles is already arranging Round 8.
I look at the clock. Two hours until noon.
I spend one of them staring at the ceiling. I spend the other washing my face, putting on clothes that aren't slept in, and rehearsing nothing, because there is no script for what comes next.
The library is cold.

Not the ambient cold of an old English house — something deliberate. The windows are open. Both of them, the tall ones facing the garden, their casements flung wide as if the room had been trying to breathe and failed. The November air pours in, carrying the smell of wet leaves and distant lake water, and the dust in the light doesn't drift. It suspends. Particles frozen mid-fall, gold and gray, like the air itself is holding its breath.
Miles is sitting in the chair nearest the hearth.
No fire. No Flora. Just him, and the cold, and the books nobody reads.

He looks different. Older. Not in the way of overnight — more like a photograph left in sunlight, the colors faded, the edges soft. There are shadows beneath his eyes I haven't seen before. His collar is unbuttoned. His hair isn't combed. For the first time since I met him, Miles Ashford looks like what he is: a sixteen-year-old boy who hasn't slept.
"You broke the rules," he says.
I stop three feet from him. I don't sit.
"There are no rules anymore. You said so yourself."
His jaw tightens. I can see the muscle move, the controlled clench of someone who has trained himself not to react. "I said no secrecy. I didn't say you could invite the whole world."
"Round seven," I say. "I went loud. That was the move."
"That was—" He stops himself. Breathes. The exhale comes out visible in the cold, a brief ghost of vapor that dissipates before it reaches me. "You don't even know what you broke."
"Tell me."

He looks at me. Really looks — not the analytical scan I've come to expect, not the game-faced appraisal of a player measuring his opponent. Something rawer. He's angry. But underneath the anger, like a current beneath ice, I can see something else.
Respect.
"You're better than the others," he says quietly.
"Than Jessel?"
The name lands between us. I watch his expression fracture — just for a second, just in the left eye, a twitch I would have missed if I hadn't been looking. He recovers. Of course he does.
"She wasn't Peter's daughter," he says. "You are. The game was always meant for you."
The words hit me the way cold water does — not painful exactly, but shocking, a full-body flinch that takes a moment to identify.
Peter's daughter. Not Henry's ward. Not the cousin who came late. Peter's daughter. The game was designed for me before I knew it existed.
"You're saying Peter —"
"I'm saying you were always the endgame." Miles looks away, toward the open window, the garden, the gray beyond. "The others were rehearsals."
*The others.* Plural. Jessel. And who before Jessel? How many rehearsals? How many women walked through this house and didn't walk out?

I should feel triumphant. He's confirming everything I suspected. But the cold is in my lungs now, and the dust is still frozen in the light, and Miles's profile against the window looks so young that it makes my teeth hurt.
"Jessel found the files," I say. "Like me."
He doesn't answer. His hand grips the arm of the chair — the left one, the one farthest from me. His knuckles whiten.
"She threatened to go public," I continue. "And then she went to the lake."

"We never hurt anyone." The words come out fast, too fast, rehearsed or primal — I can't tell which. "The Game was supposed to be psychological. That's all it ever was. Boundary testing. Compliance calibration. None of it was — we never —" His voice cracks. Actually cracks, down the middle, like a boy's voice does when it's still deciding what register to live in. "Jessel was different. She found the files, like you. She threatened to go public. And then she went to the lake. We didn't touch her. I swear we didn't touch her."
His voice breaks on the last word.

And for a moment — one moment, held in the cold and the dust and the gold-gray light — Miles Ashford is not a sociopath. He's a sixteen-year-old boy who watched a woman walk into the water and couldn't process what he was seeing. His eyes are wet. He won't let the tears fall — I can see him holding them back with the same discipline he uses for everything else — but they're there, surface tension and shame.
I don't speak. I let the silence do what silence does in this house: fill the gaps that conversation leaves.
He pulls himself together. It takes thirty seconds. I watch each one.

"We should discuss terms," he says, and his voice is level again, and I understand that this is the most terrifying thing about him — not the cruelty, but the recovery. The way he can shatter and reassemble and be functional in the space of a breath. That's not natural. That's trained. That's what Peter built.
I don't agree to terms. I leave.
The afternoon passes like a wound healing — slowly, and with itching.

I don't eat. I don't read. I sit on my bed and I think about Flora's face on the screen, the way she turned to the camera with those programmed tears, and I think about Miles in the library, the crack in his voice, the wet in his eyes, and I think about Peter — who I've never met, who might be dead, who certainly isn't here — and what it takes to make children into this.
The knock comes at four.
Soft. Hesitant. A rhythm I don't recognize — knock, pause, knock-knock. Not Miles's single rap. Not Mrs. Grose's efficient three.
I open the door.
Flora is standing in the hallway.

She's never come to my room unbidden. Not once in all the weeks I've been here. The rules of the Game — the real rules, the unspoken ones — kept her in the common spaces, the garden, the designated arenas. My room was off-limits. Sacred. A breach even the twins wouldn't commit.
Until now.
"Can I come in?"

I step aside. She enters, and she doesn't go to the chair by the window — the chair she's sat in every other time, the correct chair, the assigned position. She goes to the floor. She sits cross-legged on the rug in the center of the room, and she looks up at me, and I see how small she is. Not small the way she makes herself — coiled, compact, a body held in reserve. Small the way she actually is. Fourteen. A child in a house that taught her to be a weapon.
I sit across from her. On the floor. Eye level.
"He's lying," she says. "About Jessel."

The words come out flat. Rehearsed, but not in the way Miles rehearses. This is different. This is something she's been holding so long it's become architecture — load-bearing, structural, holding up the rest of her.

"He was at the lake that night," she continues. "I saw him. I followed him. He told me to stay in my room, but I followed him anyway, because I always — I used to —" She stops. Starts again. "He stood on the shore and watched her walk in. He didn't stop her. He didn't call out. He didn't go in after her."
Her hands are shaking. I can see them in her lap, trembling like a tuning fork struck once and left to vibrate. She presses them flat against her thighs, trying to still them, and they won't stop.
"He filmed it."
The room goes very quiet. The radiator ticks once — a metal contraction, a shift in temperature — and then nothing.

"He filmed her," Flora says again, softer now, as if the repetition might make it more bearable. It doesn't. "He stood there with his phone and he recorded her walking into the water, and he didn't do anything. He just watched. Like it was the Game. Like she was a move."

I don't reach for her. I want to — every instinct I have is screaming to take her hands, to hold them still, to tell her it's over, it's done, she's safe. But I don't know if she's safe. I don't know if touching her right now would be another performance, another compliance, another room she's been trained to navigate.
"I don't want to play anymore," she says.

Her voice is very small. Not performed small. Genuinely, terrifyingly small — the voice of someone who has just discovered that the thing she was made for is destroying her, and who has no idea what exists on the other side of that discovery.
"But I don't know how to stop."
The honesty of it demolishes me.

I sit on the floor of my room in Bly Manor with a fourteen-year-old girl who was raised to be a predator and who is asking me — *me*, the person who put her on the internet, who used her compliance as a weapon, who became what she hates — to show her an exit. And I don't have one. The Game doesn't have exits. That's the point. That's what Peter designed. A system without off-ramps, without safe harbors, without the possibility of resignation. You play until you break or you play until you die, and the house always wins.
"I'm going to figure it out," I say. I don't know if it's a lie. It feels like one. It feels like the most important lie I've ever told.
Flora nods. She doesn't look convinced. She looks exactly like what she is: a child who has been told, for the first time, that the rules might not apply — and who cannot yet believe it.

She leaves without another word. The door closes behind her, and the lock clicks — she locks it from the outside, out of habit, the way she's been trained — and then the hallway is empty and I am alone with what I know.
I have what I need.

Miles's partial confession — *we didn't touch her* — which is not an exoneration but an admission of presence, of witness, of failure to act. Flora's testimony — *he filmed it* — which is the accusation that turns negligence into participation. The recordings from the security feeds. The files from Peter's study. The Reddit archives, the screenshots, the weight of public attention that can't be entirely erased by lawyers.

I have enough to burn Bly Manor to the ground. Metaphorically. Publicly. To expose the Game, the twins, the whole architectured apparatus of cruelty that Peter built and Henry maintained and the children inherited like a disease.
But I also have something I didn't expect.
Sympathy.

Not forgiveness — I'm not there, may never be there — but something adjacent to it. Something that sits in the same cavity where my certainty used to live and whispers: *they were seven.* Peter started training them when they were seven. He taught them to read people as targets, to identify weakness as opportunity, to understand cruelty as a language and fluency as survival. They were never given a chance to be children. The Game was their cradle. Compliance was their first word.
And now I have to decide.

I can destroy them — the twins, the players, the visible faces of the system. The internet will eat them alive. Sixteen and fourteen, their names and faces and histories spread across every platform, every comment section, every true-crime podcast. They will become examples. Warnings. Sacrifices on the altar of public outrage. And they are — genuinely, biologically, indisputably — children.

Or I can destroy the Game. The structure. The architecture. The invisible machinery that made them what they are. I can go after Peter's legacy, Henry's complicity, the system that turns orphans into operators. I can name the disease instead of the symptoms.

But the Game is harder to kill. The Game has lawyers. The Game has a dead man's reputation to hide behind, a trustee's deniability, a century of Manor-protected silence. The twins are right there. Easy. Clickable. Shareable.

I sit on the floor where Flora sat, and I feel the rug beneath my palms, and I smell the cold air still bleeding through the old walls, and I listen to the silence that is thick and heavy and full of the things this house has swallowed, and I understand at last that the question is not whether I can win.
The question is whether I can win without becoming the Game's final round.
